items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences; rule utilitarianism follows rules that, if generally adopted, would produce the most good — sacrificing case-by-case calculation for predictability and fairness.
items.misconception: Act and rule utilitarianism are basically the same approach.
items.random_wrong: Act utilitarianism is about action movies and rule utilitarianism is about laws.
items.why: The distinction matters for famous counterexamples (e.g. punishing the innocent). Act utilitarianism might endorse it if outcomes are better; rule utilitarianism rejects such practices because the rule would be harmful overall.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.correct: The absolute oneness and indivisibility of Allah — the most fundamental Islamic belief. Its opposite, shirk (associating partners with God), is the gravest sin.
items.misconception: Tawhid is the Islamic word for the Trinity.
items.random_wrong: Tawhid is the name of an Islamic prayer position.
items.why: Tawhid is the strict denial of any plurality in God — directly contrasting with Trinitarian Christianity. Students sometimes invert the meaning entirely. The Quran's emphasis on Tawhid shapes Islamic theology, art (no images) and ethics.

items.q: How many sacraments does the Roman Catholic Church recognise?
items.why: It's 7. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Where is the Lord's Prayer found in Matthew's Gospel?
items.why: It's Matthew 6. “Matthew 5” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many sacraments do Catholics and Orthodox Christians recognise?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
